title: Stolpersteine in Berlin: Quick Summary
items: **The small brass squares in the pavement are Stolpersteine, "stumbling stones": one hand-made 96 mm plate for one person persecuted by the Nazis, set in front of their last freely chosen home.** Berlin has more than 11,000 of them, and the project counts more than 126,000 across 31 countries as of May 2026.

**Every stone uses the same compact formula, so you only need to learn it once.** HIER WOHNTE means "here lived", JG. is the year of birth, and the last lines tell the fate: DEPORTIERT, ERMORDET, or FLUCHT IN DEN TOD, the project's wording for a person driven to take their own life.

**Walking over them is not disrespectful; it is the design.** The artist compares them to grave slabs in cathedral floors, and footsteps keep the brass polished. The one thing not to do is stand on a stone for a posed photo.

**You will not find them at the big sights. Look down at residential doorways.** Mitte alone has about 2,200, the first Berlin stones from 1996 are in Oranienstraße in Kreuzberg, and one lies at the entrance to the Hackesche Höfe where my tour ends.
**Any stone can be looked up by name or street** on the official Berlin database and map at stolpersteine-berlin.de, or with the free Stolpersteine Berlin app.

**Anyone can propose and sponsor a stone: EUR 120, or EUR 132 from outside Germany.** The research into the person's life is the real work, and in central districts like Mitte the waiting time runs up to four years.
**Berliners polish the stones every 9 November**, the anniversary of the 1938 pogrom night. Metal polish on a cloth and water is the official method; never a wire brush.
